stakeholder_long_name: Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government (HKSAR Government)
role: Sets aging-related policy direction (e.g., ageing in place), provides subsidies/regulation and cross-bureau coordination for age-friendly implementation.

related_pain_points.label: Age-unfriendly outdoor spaces and pedestrian infrastructure
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Outdoor environment and mobility barriers for older adults: inappropriate signage, lack of open spaces/leisure facilities, inadequate outdoor seating (especially at bus stops), lack of covered pedestrian roads, short pedestrian crossing time, and traffic/driver issues.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Needs policy/ordinance integration and cross-department implementation for age-friendly built environment.

related_pain_points.label: Housing stock not age-friendly: lifts/accessibility deficits, maintenance burden, and affordability pressures
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face age-unfriendly and deteriorating housing conditions—especially in older buildings and estates without lifts or adequate age-friendly facilities, often located on hilly terrain—combined with high and ongoing maintenance costs and broader housing unaffordability. Stringent eligibility for improvement schemes leaves some (including CSSA recipients) unable to fund safety modifications, undermining ageing in place and increasing home accident risk and isolation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Policy pressure to address housing affordability/maintenance as part of age-friendly environment.

related_pain_points.label: Transport system not consistently age-friendly: lack of mandates, limited accessibility options, and worsening reliability/crowding
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Age-friendly accessibility in public transport is inconsistent due to the absence of statutory requirements and uneven operator adoption. Practical barriers include limited low-floor buses, limited barrier-free taxis/wheelchair compartments, inadequate feeder services, and poorly implemented priority seating. In addition, increased crowding and system breakdowns reduce satisfaction and constrain older adults’ mobility and participation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Policy gap on codifying accessibility requirements; must design regulation and monitoring.; Need to improve transport reliability/capacity within age-friendly planning.

related_pain_points.label: Transport affordability gaps for ages 60–64
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Lack of concessionary fares for ages 60–64 and expensive MTR fares for this group; transport costs remain a burden even with concessions, reducing motivation to go out (especially for frail wheelchair users needing taxis).
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need to subsidize and monitor concession schemes with smartcards and operator supplementation.

related_pain_points.label: Weak policy framework for implementing 'ageing in place'
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Participants note lack of policy detail/framework for how society works together to achieve ageing in place, especially for frail older adults.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Needs clearer priorities, measurable outcomes, and cross-bureau coordination to operationalize policy.

related_pain_points.label: Ageism and declining respect/social inclusion
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults perceived as neglected/disrespected; youth not always respectful; elderly abuse occurs (often by family) with unreported cases; some malls exclude older adults; few age-friendly facilities in malls.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need to address ageism and social inclusion through education, public messaging and protective policy mechanisms.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate retirement protection (MPF insufficient)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Financial protection under MPF 'is not enough' for decent retirement; many older adults have little income and no MPF protection, avoiding activities to save money for essentials (e.g., medical consultation).
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Faces calls for structural policy measures (e.g., universal retirement protection).

related_pain_points.label: Limited volunteer options and lack of incentives for older volunteers
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older volunteer services limited in scope; inadequate information; lack of financial incentives (e.g., transport allowance); no comprehensive strategy; government support mostly routed to NGOs rather than directly to volunteers.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need long-term plan, indicators, and direct support mechanisms.

related_pain_points.label: Age discrimination and limited employability supports for older workers
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Age discrimination in labor market; older workers not covered under employees’ compensation insurance reduces employability; lack of tailored retraining and limited employment statistics; gender pay disparities among older workers.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need policies/incentives (tax/insurance) and improved data to extend working age.

related_pain_points.label: Employment loss and widening health inequalities during COVID-19
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Pandemic containment led to employment loss, isolation, and loneliness, aggravating existing health inequalities affecting older adults.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need for targeted socioeconomic policies to counter disparities during crises.

related_pain_points.label: Lack of measurable outcomes and data transparency for age-friendly initiatives
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: No measurable policy outcomes identified under the age-friendly framework; difficulties allocating updated data encountered; need regular databases and transparency so public can evaluate objectives.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Reduced accountability and difficulty setting priorities and targets across bureaus.

role: Operates public hospital/clinic services used by older adults; implicated in coordination, repeat examinations, and chronic-care pathways.

related_pain_points.label: Fragmented health–social care integration and poor continuity across sectors
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Health and community care are poorly integrated across bureaus, departments, and provider tiers. Coordination and communication failures lead to duplicated examinations, recurrent referrals, wasted resources, and lack of seamless pathways. Continuity is weak (rotating doctors/doctor shopping), and planning for new services can be inconsistent—creating fragmented journeys and poorer outcomes for older adults with chronic needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Operational inefficiencies from poor information exchange and repeated examinations.; Unnecessary demand and inefficiencies (duplication/overlap), increasing wait times.

related_pain_points.label: Health service access friction and user-unfriendly appointment systems
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face practical access barriers in health services due to misaligned district quotas, separated service locations (consultations vs dispensaries) that increase travel, and appointment/booking systems that limit choice and are not user-oriented for sensory or cognitive difficulties. Technical failures and rescheduling difficulties further reduce timely access to primary and preventive care.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Faces downstream impacts of access inefficiencies and chronic disease management gaps.; Higher call friction, missed appointments, and inefficiencies in appointment management.

related_pain_points.label: Hospital capacity strain: inpatient crowding and long waits for public services
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Public hospital services are overwhelmed, leading to inpatient crowding and long waiting times (including A&E crowding and very long specialist outpatient waits). Demand exceeding supply reduces perceived quality and timeliness of care and increases burden on families coordinating repeated visits.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Staff workload increases; service quality and throughput undermined.; Operational strain due to demand exceeding supply; capacity management challenges.

related_pain_points.label: Hospital-centric system and inadequate redesign to meet rising frailty burden
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: The health and social care system is not adequately designed for the growing frailty and dependency burden. Primary care is underdeveloped and the system relies heavily on acute hospitals; many admissions could be prevented or managed through stronger primary care, early detection/screening, and improved lower-tier chronic care—contributing to escalating demand pressures.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Increased demand pressure and inpatient crowding, stressing service capacity.; Avoidable admissions and outpatient overload increase, worsening waits and workforce strain.

related_pain_points.label: Limited discharge support and transitional care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Discharge support services are relatively limited; ICDS targets only high-risk readmission patients, leaving many without home care support; long waits for day care (6+ months) lead to use of private residential homes as immediate alternatives.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Readmission risk and extended care demand persists when community follow-up is inadequate.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate end-of-life care capacity and lack of holistic framework
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Many older people die in acute wards lacking privacy/dignity and spiritual/holistic care; palliative single rooms and units are inadequate; no overarching framework addressing holistic patient and family needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Acute wards take on end-of-life demand; capacity mismatch in palliative units.

related_pain_points.label: Legal uncertainty around advance directives
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Hong Kong lacks statute/case law for advance directives; reliance on common-law informed consent creates practical difficulties and potential conflicts, prompting need for legal framework/public consultation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Operational ambiguity and risk management challenges implementing ADs.

role: Provides community support services and community mental health services for older adults; part of cross-system coordination needs.

related_pain_points.label: Insufficient and uneven community support for frail older adults and caregivers (ageing-in-place shortfalls)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Despite an ageing-in-place policy direction, community support capacity and quality are insufficient relative to frailty and ADL/IADL impairment. Gaps include inadequate quotas and coverage (home help, outreach, escort, day care, end-of-life support), limited accessibility for remote areas, weak promotion, and stringent eligibility—shifting burden onto family caregivers and increasing risk of avoidable institutionalization.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: System strain and need for expanded community care and integrated service models.

related_pain_points.label: Long waits for subsidized residential care and inadequate caregiver support
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Caregiver needs are not adequately met in the long-term care system, highlighted by long waiting times for residential care home services. High demand and manpower constraints contribute to prolonged waits for subsidized places, increasing caregiver stress and leaving some older adults without timely placement when needed.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Capacity and service design pressure to reduce waiting times and expand alternatives.; Central Waiting List grows; reputational/policy pressure to expand and staff the sector.

related_pain_points.label: Fragmented health–social care integration and poor continuity across sectors
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Health and community care are poorly integrated across bureaus, departments, and provider tiers. Coordination and communication failures lead to duplicated examinations, recurrent referrals, wasted resources, and lack of seamless pathways. Continuity is weak (rotating doctors/doctor shopping), and planning for new services can be inconsistent—creating fragmented journeys and poorer outcomes for older adults with chronic needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Fragmented interface with medical services undermines community support effectiveness.; Long-term care pathways affected by lack of cohesive linkage with medical sector.

related_pain_points.label: Imbalanced funding: residential care prioritized over community care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Expenditure and places skew toward residential care vs community care (2014/15: HK$3.9b for 26,325 residential places vs HK$1.1b for 9,680 community places), undermining 'ageing in place' and contributing to high institutionalization.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Policy incoherence vs stated ageing-in-place direction; pressure to revisit financing models.

related_pain_points.label: Weak quality assurance in private residential care homes
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Private RCHE quality varies; many only meet minimum standards under an outdated ordinance (Cap.459 from 1996); voluntary accreditation uptake is low (61 of 738), indicating need for updated statutory requirements and compulsory accreditation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Limited assurance over sector quality unless schemes (EBPS/contract) apply.

related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face practical access barriers in health services due to misaligned district quotas, separated service locations (consultations vs dispensaries) that increase travel, and appointment/booking systems that limit choice and are not user-oriented for sensory or cognitive difficulties. related_pain_points.description_pain_point: The health and social care system is not adequately designed for the growing frailty and dependency burden. Primary care is underdeveloped and the system relies heavily on acute hospitals; many admissions could be prevented or managed through stronger primary care, early detection/screening, and improved lower-tier chronic care—contributing to escalating demand pressures.

level2_reasoning: Key actors in policy-making and governance structures.

stakeholder_long_name: Community-living older people in Hong Kong (aged 50 years and over)
role: Primary population whose quality of life, health, participation, and wellbeing are measured and affected by policies, unrest, and pandemics.

related_pain_points.label: Age-unfriendly outdoor spaces and pedestrian infrastructure
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Outdoor environment and mobility barriers for older adults: inappropriate signage, lack of open spaces/leisure facilities, inadequate outdoor seating (especially at bus stops), lack of covered pedestrian roads, short pedestrian crossing time, and traffic/driver issues.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Reduced walkability and safety; difficulty navigating and crossing roads; constrained outdoor activity and wellbeing.

related_pain_points.label: Housing stock not age-friendly: lifts/accessibility deficits, maintenance burden, and affordability pressures
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face age-unfriendly and deteriorating housing conditions—especially in older buildings and estates without lifts or adequate age-friendly facilities, often located on hilly terrain—combined with high and ongoing maintenance costs and broader housing unaffordability. Stringent eligibility for improvement schemes leaves some (including CSSA recipients) unable to fund safety modifications, undermining ageing in place and increasing home accident risk and isolation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Mobility barriers and isolation risk; difficulty accessing services and community life, undermining ageing in place.; Financial insecurity and inability to make safety/age-friendly modifications; heightened accident risk at home.; Lower satisfaction with housing; financial stress and reduced quality of life.

related_pain_points.label: Lack of tailor-made age-friendly housing due to market orientation
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Shortage of tailor-made housing for older adults attributed to 'market driven orientations of land developers'; Senior Citizen Residences marketed mainly to middle-class; lack of age-friendly facilities in public housing.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Limited suitable housing options across socio-economic groups; barriers to ageing in place.

related_pain_points.label: Transport system not consistently age-friendly: lack of mandates, limited accessibility options, and worsening reliability/crowding
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Age-friendly accessibility in public transport is inconsistent due to the absence of statutory requirements and uneven operator adoption. Practical barriers include limited low-floor buses, limited barrier-free taxis/wheelchair compartments, inadequate feeder services, and poorly implemented priority seating. In addition, increased crowding and system breakdowns reduce satisfaction and constrain older adults’ mobility and participation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Uneven accessibility experience across operators; barriers to mobility and participation.; Difficulty traveling safely and comfortably; reduced independence and participation.; Reduced mobility and satisfaction with public transport; barriers to participation.

related_pain_points.label: Transport affordability gaps for ages 60–64
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Lack of concessionary fares for ages 60–64 and expensive MTR fares for this group; transport costs remain a burden even with concessions, reducing motivation to go out (especially for frail wheelchair users needing taxis).
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Lower social participation and access to services due to cost barriers; heightened isolation risk.

related_pain_points.label: Insufficient and uneven community support for frail older adults and caregivers (ageing-in-place shortfalls)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Despite an ageing-in-place policy direction, community support capacity and quality are insufficient relative to frailty and ADL/IADL impairment. Gaps include inadequate quotas and coverage (home help, outreach, escort, day care, end-of-life support), limited accessibility for remote areas, weak promotion, and stringent eligibility—shifting burden onto family caregivers and increasing risk of avoidable institutionalization.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Unmet daily support needs; increased risk of institutionalization and reduced quality of life.; Reduced access/quality of home-help, outreach, escort and day care support; harder to age in place, especially in remote areas.

related_pain_points.label: Long waits for subsidized residential care and inadequate caregiver support
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Caregiver needs are not adequately met in the long-term care system, highlighted by long waiting times for residential care home services. High demand and manpower constraints contribute to prolonged waits for subsidized places, increasing caregiver stress and leaving some older adults without timely placement when needed.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: May be pushed toward residential care as 'only choice' despite preference to avoid it.; Delayed access to needed residential care; some die while waiting.

related_pain_points.label: Weak policy framework for implementing 'ageing in place'
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Participants note lack of policy detail/framework for how society works together to achieve ageing in place, especially for frail older adults.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Inconsistent access to supports needed to remain in community safely.

related_pain_points.label: Barriers to social participation: information, distance, and affordability
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Socially disengaged older adults lack information on activities/help; some live far from elderly centers; affordability affects ability to participate; poor neighborhood relations in urban communities.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Lower participation, weaker networks, and higher isolation risk.

related_pain_points.label: Ageism and declining respect/social inclusion
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults perceived as neglected/disrespected; youth not always respectful; elderly abuse occurs (often by family) with unreported cases; some malls exclude older adults; few age-friendly facilities in malls.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Reduced dignity, participation and belonging; heightened vulnerability to abuse and exclusion.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate retirement protection (MPF insufficient)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Financial protection under MPF 'is not enough' for decent retirement; many older adults have little income and no MPF protection, avoiding activities to save money for essentials (e.g., medical consultation).
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Economic insecurity and constrained social/health choices.

related_pain_points.label: Limited volunteer options and lack of incentives for older volunteers
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older volunteer services limited in scope; inadequate information; lack of financial incentives (e.g., transport allowance); no comprehensive strategy; government support mostly routed to NGOs rather than directly to volunteers.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Underutilized skills/expertise and lower civic participation.

related_pain_points.label: Age discrimination and limited employability supports for older workers
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Age discrimination in labor market; older workers not covered under employees’ compensation insurance reduces employability; lack of tailored retraining and limited employment statistics; gender pay disparities among older workers.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Fewer job opportunities and insecure income, especially for low/no-skill workers during downturns.

related_pain_points.label: Digital and communication exclusion of older adults (including frail users)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults—especially frail, low-literacy, blind, cognitively impaired, or low-income individuals—are excluded by digital-first and automated communication channels. Service information is often online-only; many lack device skills or capacity and may not afford costs. Automated systems without human support are confusing; printed alternatives are limited; NGO ICT access is constrained; and broadcast content may be too fast/complex—reducing service uptake and remote support access.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Information isolation, reduced service uptake, and difficulty navigating automated service channels.; Digital divide reduces capability domain outcomes and access to services.

related_pain_points.label: Fragmented health–social care integration and poor continuity across sectors
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Health and community care are poorly integrated across bureaus, departments, and provider tiers. Coordination and communication failures lead to duplicated examinations, recurrent referrals, wasted resources, and lack of seamless pathways. Continuity is weak (rotating doctors/doctor shopping), and planning for new services can be inconsistent—creating fragmented journeys and poorer outcomes for older adults with chronic needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Time-consuming care journeys, duplicative tests, fragmented support and poorer outcomes for chronic conditions.; More clinic/hospital visits, duplicated assessments, longer stays, poorer experience and outcomes.

related_pain_points.label: Health service access friction and user-unfriendly appointment systems
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face practical access barriers in health services due to misaligned district quotas, separated service locations (consultations vs dispensaries) that increase travel, and appointment/booking systems that limit choice and are not user-oriented for sensory or cognitive difficulties. Technical failures and rescheduling difficulties further reduce timely access to primary and preventive care.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Greater burden especially for multi-morbidity; increased travel and reduced timely access.; Reduced ability to access primary care appointments and manage care plans independently.

related_pain_points.label: Hospital capacity strain: inpatient crowding and long waits for public services
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Public hospital services are overwhelmed, leading to inpatient crowding and long waiting times (including A&E crowding and very long specialist outpatient waits). Demand exceeding supply reduces perceived quality and timeliness of care and increases burden on families coordinating repeated visits.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Delayed consultations/treatments and reduced quality due to shortened consultation time.; Delays/access barriers and crowding reduce perceived quality and timeliness of care.

related_pain_points.label: Hospital-centric system and inadequate redesign to meet rising frailty burden
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: The health and social care system is not adequately designed for the growing frailty and dependency burden. Primary care is underdeveloped and the system relies heavily on acute hospitals; many admissions could be prevented or managed through stronger primary care, early detection/screening, and improved lower-tier chronic care—contributing to escalating demand pressures.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Unmet care needs and declining self-rated health, wellbeing, and functional outcomes.; Chronic conditions and geriatric syndromes receive fragmented episodic care rather than continuous community management.

related_pain_points.label: Limited discharge support and transitional care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Discharge support services are relatively limited; ICDS targets only high-risk readmission patients, leaving many without home care support; long waits for day care (6+ months) lead to use of private residential homes as immediate alternatives.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Higher risk of poor transitions, reduced independence, and being diverted to private residential homes.

related_pain_points.label: Imbalanced funding: residential care prioritized over community care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Expenditure and places skew toward residential care vs community care (2014/15: HK$3.9b for 26,325 residential places vs HK$1.1b for 9,680 community places), undermining 'ageing in place' and contributing to high institutionalization.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Fewer comprehensive community options, increasing reliance on institutional care even when home ageing is feasible.

related_pain_points.label: Weak quality assurance in private residential care homes
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Private RCHE quality varies; many only meet minimum standards under an outdated ordinance (Cap.459 from 1996); voluntary accreditation uptake is low (61 of 738), indicating need for updated statutory requirements and compulsory accreditation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Greater uncertainty and safety/quality risks when diverted to private homes due to public shortages.

related_pain_points.label: High and opaque private-sector costs and voucher limitations
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Dissatisfaction with private medical/dental costs and fee transparency; EHCV amount sometimes insufficient for dental/general expenses; claims that some private providers increase charges for voucher users.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Financial barriers persist even with vouchers; reduced ability to access private options.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate end-of-life care capacity and lack of holistic framework
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Many older people die in acute wards lacking privacy/dignity and spiritual/holistic care; palliative single rooms and units are inadequate; no overarching framework addressing holistic patient and family needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: End-of-life experience often in crowded unfamiliar acute settings, not aligned with 'good death' principles.

related_pain_points.label: Legal uncertainty around advance directives
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Hong Kong lacks statute/case law for advance directives; reliance on common-law informed consent creates practical difficulties and potential conflicts, prompting need for legal framework/public consultation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Reduced certainty that preferences will be respected when incapacitated.

related_pain_points.label: Pandemic containment measures driving isolation and frailty
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: COVID-19 and containment measures (closures of centres, restrictions on gatherings, cordoned exercise areas) contributed to isolation/loneliness and deterioration in frailty among older adults.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Increased isolation and loneliness; reduced social participation and wellbeing during restrictions.

related_pain_points.label: Mental health burden from social unrest and pandemic
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Political conflicts/social unrest and COVID-19 coincided with marked drops in mental health and life satisfaction indicators; unrest linked to depression/PTSD burden and family conflicts.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Declines in mental health and subjective wellbeing; increased stress and insecurity.

related_pain_points.label: Employment loss and widening health inequalities during COVID-19
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Pandemic containment led to employment loss, isolation, and loneliness, aggravating existing health inequalities affecting older adults.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Reduced income security and increased vulnerability; heightened inequality effects.

role: Provide informal support; affected by lack of caregiver support services and long waiting times for residential care.

related_pain_points.label: Insufficient and uneven community support for frail older adults and caregivers (ageing-in-place shortfalls)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Despite an ageing-in-place policy direction, community support capacity and quality are insufficient relative to frailty and ADL/IADL impairment. Gaps include inadequate quotas and coverage (home help, outreach, escort, day care, end-of-life support), limited accessibility for remote areas, weak promotion, and stringent eligibility—shifting burden onto family caregivers and increasing risk of avoidable institutionalization.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Greater caregiving burden and stress; limited formal supports.; Higher caregiving burden and stress due to insufficient support/respite and difficult eligibility.

related_pain_points.label: Long waits for subsidized residential care and inadequate caregiver support
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Caregiver needs are not adequately met in the long-term care system, highlighted by long waiting times for residential care home services. High demand and manpower constraints contribute to prolonged waits for subsidized places, increasing caregiver stress and leaving some older adults without timely placement when needed.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Increased stress and burden managing frailty without adequate formal support.

related_pain_points.label: Hospital capacity strain: inpatient crowding and long waits for public services
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Public hospital services are overwhelmed, leading to inpatient crowding and long waiting times (including A&E crowding and very long specialist outpatient waits). Demand exceeding supply reduces perceived quality and timeliness of care and increases burden on families coordinating repeated visits.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: More time spent coordinating care and accompanying elders; stress from delays and repeated visits.

related_pain_points.label: Limited discharge support and transitional care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Discharge support services are relatively limited; ICDS targets only high-risk readmission patients, leaving many without home care support; long waits for day care (6+ months) lead to use of private residential homes as immediate alternatives.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Increased burden immediately after discharge; more coordination and care tasks.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate end-of-life care capacity and lack of holistic framework
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Many older people die in acute wards lacking privacy/dignity and spiritual/holistic care; palliative single rooms and units are inadequate; no overarching framework addressing holistic patient and family needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Emotional distress and limited support/visiting flexibility during final stage.

related_pain_points.label: Legal uncertainty around advance directives
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Hong Kong lacks statute/case law for advance directives; reliance on common-law informed consent creates practical difficulties and potential conflicts, prompting need for legal framework/public consultation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Greater decision burden and potential conflict with providers during end-of-life decisions.

related_pain_points.label: Age-unfriendly outdoor spaces and pedestrian infrastructure
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Outdoor environment and mobility barriers for older adults: inappropriate signage, lack of open spaces/leisure facilities, inadequate outdoor seating (especially at bus stops), lack of covered pedestrian roads, short pedestrian crossing time, and traffic/driver issues.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Pressure to follow up minor works and district upgrades; need coordination with elderly groups.

related_pain_points.label: Housing stock not age-friendly: lifts/accessibility deficits, maintenance burden, and affordability pressures
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults face age-unfriendly and deteriorating housing conditions—especially in older buildings and estates without lifts or adequate age-friendly facilities, often located on hilly terrain—combined with high and ongoing maintenance costs and broader housing unaffordability. Stringent eligibility for improvement schemes leaves some (including CSSA recipients) unable to fund safety modifications, undermining ageing in place and increasing home accident risk and isolation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: District-level infrastructure adjustments (e.g., bridges/lifts) require coordination and funding prioritization.

related_pain_points.label: Transport system not consistently age-friendly: lack of mandates, limited accessibility options, and worsening reliability/crowding
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Age-friendly accessibility in public transport is inconsistent due to the absence of statutory requirements and uneven operator adoption. Practical barriers include limited low-floor buses, limited barrier-free taxis/wheelchair compartments, inadequate feeder services, and poorly implemented priority seating. In addition, increased crowding and system breakdowns reduce satisfaction and constrain older adults’ mobility and participation.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Local planning needed for feeder services and accessible station environments.

related_pain_points.label: Limited volunteer options and lack of incentives for older volunteers
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older volunteer services limited in scope; inadequate information; lack of financial incentives (e.g., transport allowance); no comprehensive strategy; government support mostly routed to NGOs rather than directly to volunteers.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need diversification of roles, information flow, and recognition schemes.

related_pain_points.label: Digital and communication exclusion of older adults (including frail users)
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Older adults—especially frail, low-literacy, blind, cognitively impaired, or low-income individuals—are excluded by digital-first and automated communication channels. Service information is often online-only; many lack device skills or capacity and may not afford costs. Automated systems without human support are confusing; printed alternatives are limited; NGO ICT access is constrained; and broadcast content may be too fast/complex—reducing service uptake and remote support access.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Operational constraints in supporting older adults due to limited ICT resources; need training and ambassador models.

related_pain_points.label: Fragmented health–social care integration and poor continuity across sectors
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Health and community care are poorly integrated across bureaus, departments, and provider tiers. Coordination and communication failures lead to duplicated examinations, recurrent referrals, wasted resources, and lack of seamless pathways. Continuity is weak (rotating doctors/doctor shopping), and planning for new services can be inconsistent—creating fragmented journeys and poorer outcomes for older adults with chronic needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Harder to coordinate services and referrals; higher admin burden.

related_pain_points.label: Limited discharge support and transitional care
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Discharge support services are relatively limited; ICDS targets only high-risk readmission patients, leaving many without home care support; long waits for day care (6+ months) lead to use of private residential homes as immediate alternatives.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need to fill discharge and home support gaps with limited capacity/coordination.

related_pain_points.label: Inadequate end-of-life care capacity and lack of holistic framework
related_pain_points.description_pain_point: Many older people die in acute wards lacking privacy/dignity and spiritual/holistic care; palliative single rooms and units are inadequate; no overarching framework addressing holistic patient and family needs.
related_pain_points.pain_point_impact_text: Need to supplement bereavement/hospice support without system-wide framework.

related_pain_points.label: Pandemic containment measures driving isolation and frailty
